Q1: I usually tried to spread my studying across the week, aiming for about 10 hours total. Being a kinesthetic learner, I found it hard to just sit and read for hours, so I'd break things up with hands-on practice or walking around while reviewing notes. I didn't have the most rigid schedule, but I tried to keep Sunday afternoons and a few weekday evenings dedicated to coursework. It wasn't perfect, but it worked well enough for me to stay on track most of the time.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I tried to maintain some consistency, like logging in a few times a week, but when assignments were coming up, I'd shift into a more intense mode. Life stuff would sometimes throw me off too—work commitments or just needing a mental break. But overall, I'd say I was somewhere in the middle between totally structured and completely reactive.
Q3: Honestly, I'd usually log in when I remembered something was due or when I wanted to check if there were new announcements. Sometimes I'd get a notification or a message from a classmate in our peer group that would remind me to hop on. I also liked checking the discussion boards to see what others were saying, which became kind of a habit. It wasn't always about assignments—sometimes I just wanted to feel connected to the course.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks, logging in became pretty automatic for me. I think the peer support setup really helped because I'd check in to see if my study buddies had posted anything or needed help. Watching videos became part of my evening wind-down routine—I'd put one on while eating dinner sometimes. Once it felt like part of my day rather than an extra task, it was much easier to stay engaged.
Q5: The video content was really valuable to me—I watched a ton of it because I could pause, rewind, and actually work through examples at my own pace. I also found the discussion posts helpful because hearing how other students understood concepts sometimes clicked for me in ways the lectures didn't. The peer interactions were great too; having that support network made me feel less alone in the struggle.
Q6: I'll admit I didn't always complete every reading or supplementary material they posted. Some of the PDFs felt really dense, and as someone who learns better by doing, I'd skim them and move on. Optional quizzes I sometimes skipped if I felt confident enough, which maybe wasn't the smartest move. I tended to prioritize things that felt immediately applicable to assignments over background readings.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to what I needed to do for assignments or exams. If it was interactive or let me practice something hands-on, I was way more likely to engage with it fully. I also valued things that helped me understand concepts I was struggling with—like when a video finally made something click. Feeling like I was making progress made it worthwhile.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—there was less pressure, which was nice, but also less motivation to actually do them. Required activities had this urgency that pushed me to complete them even when I wasn't feeling it. With optional stuff, I'd often think 'I'll get to that later' and then never did. But sometimes the optional discussions were actually more interesting because people were there by choice.
Q9: Oh definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos to review offline, especially when I wanted to study somewhere without good internet or just away from my computer. I also kept a physical notebook where I'd work through problems by hand—that kinesthetic aspect really helped me retain information. Sometimes I'd use YouTube videos or other websites to supplement what we were learning in class.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was studying hard but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like when I'd print out practice problems and work through them at a coffee shop, or when I'd meet up with my peer support group in person to quiz each other. I also spent time just thinking through concepts while walking or doing chores—processing information doesn't always happen in front of a screen.
Q11: Yes, all the time actually. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download the slides, and then review them multiple times offline over the following days. Same with readings—I'd access them once to download, then read them on my tablet without being connected. The LMS would only show that initial access, not all the studying that came after.
Q12: There were a couple of weeks mid-semester where my motivation really dipped. Nothing major was happening, I just felt kind of burnt out and disconnected from the material. My attendance slipped a bit during that time, and I fell behind on some assignments. The peer support actually helped pull me out of it—having people check in on me made a difference.
Q13: Definitely. When I had a lot going on outside of school, my LMS usage would drop even if I was still trying to keep up with the material in other ways. Fatigue was a big one—after a long day, I'd sometimes just skim through things without really absorbing them. Competing responsibilities meant I had to prioritize, and sometimes the course took a backseat even though I didn't want it to.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be logged in, scrolling through materials, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd watch a video but realize at the end I hadn't absorbed anything. It's frustrating because the system would show I was active, but I wasn't really learning in those moments. Usually happened when I was tired or stressed about other things.
Q15: I definitely procrastinated on some assignments, especially the ones that felt overwhelming or unclear. Even knowing they were important, I'd put them off because starting felt hard. Sometimes I avoided discussion posts when I wasn't confident about my understanding—I didn't want to say something wrong in front of everyone. The peer support helped with this because I could ask questions privately first.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me. My LMS activity would spike in the days before something was due—I'd be watching videos, reviewing materials, checking discussions for hints. Without deadlines, I'd probably drift too much. They gave me structure even when my internal motivation was low. I learned to use them as anchors for my study schedule.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it definitely motivated me to engage more. Seeing that my work was on the right track made me want to keep going. Constructive criticism helped too because it gave me direction for what to focus on. I'd usually log in pretty quickly after grades were posted to see how I did and what I needed to improve.
Q18: The course structure was pretty clear, which helped me plan my time. Having regular deadlines kept me accountable, and the way modules were organized made it easy to know what to focus on each week. The peer support component was really beneficial for me—it added a social element that made the course feel less isolating. I appreciated having that built into the structure.
Q19: The video content being available on-demand made engagement much easier for me since I could fit it around my schedule. The discussion boards were well-organized, which made participating less intimidating. What made it harder was when assignment instructions were vague—I'd spend too much time trying to figure out what was expected instead of actually doing the work. More examples would have helped.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to understand the material but struggling to actually produce work that meets the requirements. They might be watching videos repeatedly, reading discussions, searching for clarity—but when it comes to assignments, they freeze up. It could also be anxiety about submitting imperfect work, or just not managing time well enough to complete things.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My exam scores and assignment completion weren't amazing, but I engaged a lot with videos and discussions. Some students might be efficient—they know exactly what they need, get it done quickly, and don't need to spend hours in the LMS. Or they're studying effectively offline. High LMS activity doesn't automatically mean better outcomes.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns pretty well—when you're active, what you're clicking on, how long you spend on things. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually understanding the material or just going through the motions. They also miss all the offline studying, the conversations with peers, the thinking that happens away from the screen.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what I'm learning and putting in effort to understand it—not just checking boxes. It's about being curious, asking questions, connecting with classmates, and feeling like the material matters. It's showing up mentally, not just physically or digitally. Some of my most engaged moments weren't visible online at all.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much the peer support meant to me emotionally—how those connections kept me going when motivation was low. It wouldn't capture the satisfaction I felt when something finally clicked, or the informal study sessions with classmates. It also wouldn't show that even when my activity looked steady, my internal experience varied a lot from week to week.
